summary: An arithmetic sequence adds a constant each step; a geometric sequence multiplies by a constant each step.
explanation: In an arithmetic sequence, each term differs from the previous one by a constant "common difference" \(d\); the \(n\)th term is \(a_1+(n-1)d\). The sum of the first \(n\) terms is the average of the first and last term, times the number of terms: \(S_n = \frac{n}{2}(a_1+a_n)\). In a geometric sequence, each term is the previous one multiplied by a constant ratio \(r\); the \(n\)th term is \(a_1 \times r^{n-1}\). To identify which type of sequence you're given, check whether consecutive differences are constant (arithmetic) or consecutive ratios are constant (geometric). A common trap is off-by-one errors in the exponent/multiplier — remember the first term uses \(n=1\), so the exponent or multiplier count is always \(n-1\), not \(n\).
